context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigations — Case Study Stressors

Recommended due date: June 20 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, and will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners, for peer learning.

Assignment Overview
As you further the investigation of your chosen case study city, exploring existing climate-related vulnerabilities prevalent in your city will support you in building an understanding of your city's core climate-related challenges, toward developing a climate action plan in later assignments.

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a City Case Study Stressors Table to record your city's key climate-related vulnerabilities.

Step 1: Identify Vulnerabilities Across Multiple Sectors
Consider vulnerabilities across the following sectors:
• Environmental
• Social
• Health
• Infrastructure
• Culture

Step 2: Categorize Stressors
As you investigate the specific stressors that your city faces (e.g. coastal flooding, air quality, extreme heat), list them in your table based on whether the stressor is:
• Environmental
• Social
• Health
• Infrastructure
• Culture-related
• Or a combination of multiple sectors

Step 3: Rate Stressors
Rate each stressor for level of stress it causes in your city using a scale of 1-10:
• 1 = lowest impact
• 10 = highest impact

Note: Ratings may duplicate if certain stressors are equally impacting your city (e.g. coastal flooding and sea level rise might both be rated as 10).

Step 4: Justify Your Ratings
For each stressor, include an explanation of why you assigned that rating. What evidence or conditions support this severity level?

Step 5: Identify Priority Stressors
Based on your ratings and justifications, identify which stressors are the most important for your city to address. Consider both severity and urgency.

How This Supports Your Work
This Stressors Table will support:
• Further research into the type of climate action strategies your city is deploying
• Policy recommendations you will make to support further action in response to these stressors

Resources
See the example below of a completed City Case Study Stressors Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m working on the City Case Study Stressors assignment for my climate action course. I picked a case study city, but I’m kind of stuck on how to organize the stressors across sectors and rate them without feeling like I’m just guessing.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—your problem is not the city, it’s your method. So build the method first.

Start with 3 columns only: stressor, sector(s), evidence for severity. Before rating anything, ask yourself: what concrete signs make a stressor “high” in this city—frequency, number of people affected, damage, disruption, health burden, or inequity?

What city did you choose, and can you name 3 possible stressors you’ve already found?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will keep the response brief and use guided Socratic questions so the student develops their own framework for organizing and rating stressors. I will not provide a filled solution or write the assignment for them. Instead, I will direct them to the assignment’s required sectors, rating task, and justification step, and ask a focused question that moves them toward building their own table with evidence-based criteria.

exchanges.student: I chose Jakarta. Three stressors I’ve found are flooding/coastal flooding, extreme heat, and air pollution. I think flooding is environmental + infrastructure + social, but I’m less sure how to sort the other two.
exchanges.tutor:
That flooding classification is a solid start.

For the other two, don’t ask “what box fits best?” Ask: what does this stressor harm in Jakarta? People’s bodies, daily life, built systems, ecosystems, cultural practices?

Take extreme heat first: which 2–3 sectors does it clearly affect in Jakarta, and what specific impacts make you say that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student’s valid start, keep the response concise, and use Socratic prompts that help them classify stressors by tracing who or what is affected. I will avoid giving a final categorization and instead ask targeted questions that lead them to justify multi-sector labels from the assignment’s sector list.

exchanges.student: I’d say extreme heat affects health for sure, and maybe infrastructure and social too. Health because of heat stress and maybe worse conditions for older people or outdoor workers; infrastructure maybe because energy demand goes up and transport or buildings get stressed, and social because poorer neighborhoods probably have less cooling.
exchanges.tutor:
Better. That’s the kind of cross-sector reasoning the table wants.

Now tighten it: which of those impacts in Jakarta seems most direct and most severe? That should drive the rating. Don’t list everything equally.

If you had to justify an extreme heat rating with 2 pieces of evidence or conditions, what would you point to?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will reinforce the student's reasoning, then push them toward evidence-based rating criteria rather than broad labels. To follow the assignment, I will ask a focused question about how they would justify severity in Jakarta using observable conditions, without supplying the answer.

exchanges.student: I think the most direct one is health, especially for outdoor workers and low-income residents who can’t avoid exposure as easily. For evidence/conditions, I’d point to Jakarta’s hot humid climate making heat harder to tolerate, and the urban heat island effect from dense built-up areas.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now you’re no longer guessing; you’re using conditions.

Next step: relative ranking. Compared with flooding in Jakarta, is extreme heat clearly lower, about similar, or nearly as urgent? Why?

Give flooding and extreme heat provisional 1–10 ratings now, even if rough.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will validate the student’s progress and guide them to convert qualitative reasoning into a numerical rating by comparing stressors using severity and urgency, as required by the assignment. I will avoid assigning the number myself and instead ask a bounded comparative question that moves directly toward completing the table.

exchanges.student: I’d rate flooding around 9 or 10, because it seems like a really major recurring problem in Jakarta and it affects homes, transport, and probably displacement too. Extreme heat feels lower, maybe 6 or 7, because it’s serious but I don’t think it disrupts the whole city as dramatically as flooding.
exchanges.tutor:
That logic is much stronger than guessing: recurrence + scale of disruption + who is affected.

Now apply the same test to air pollution. Which sectors does it hit most directly in Jakarta, and would you place it closer to flooding or closer to extreme heat in severity?

Give me a provisional sector list and a rough rating.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will confirm the student’s comparative logic and move them to the remaining stressor using the same method. I will not supply classifications or ratings, but ask a targeted question that helps them justify air pollution by sectors and compare it to the two existing stressors, keeping the response brief and solution-oriented.

exchanges.student: I think air pollution is mainly health and environmental, and maybe social too because exposure probably isn’t equal across the city. I’d put it closer to extreme heat than flooding, maybe a 7 or 8, since it can affect a lot of people but maybe not as suddenly as flooding.
exchanges.tutor:
Reasonable, but be precise: chronic widespread harm can rival dramatic disruption. Don’t underrate a stressor just because it’s less sudden.

For air pollution, what 2 conditions in Jakarta make a 7–8 defensible? Then rank your top priority: flooding, air pollution, or extreme heat—and justify that choice in one sentence.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will validate the student's emerging framework and push them to sharpen distinctions using the assignment’s criteria of severity and urgency. I will avoid providing final ratings or wording, and instead ask for concise justification and prioritization so the student can build their own table entries.

exchanges.student: For air pollution, I’d use that it’s widespread and repeated exposure, and that it creates a direct health burden, especially respiratory problems. My top priority is still flooding, because in Jakarta it seems to combine very high severity with immediate disruption to homes, transport, and basic city functioning.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. You now have the core logic for the table.

Next, integrate it yourself: for each of the 3 stressors, write 1 line with sector(s), rating, and the specific condition/evidence behind the rating.

Start with flooding. What’s your full row draft in your own words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will acknowledge the student's progress, keep the response bite-sized, and use guided discovery to push them toward assembling the required table and justification without writing it for them. I will not provide completed entries, but I will prompt the student to synthesize their own work in the assignment's structure.

exchanges.student: Flooding/coastal flooding — Environmental, infrastructure, and social — 10 — I gave it a 10 because it’s a recurring major stressor in Jakarta that disrupts transport, damages homes and infrastructure, and affects large numbers of residents, especially in vulnerable areas.
exchanges.tutor:
Strong start. One weakness: “vulnerable areas” is still vague. What makes those areas vulnerable in Jakarta?

Now do the same for air pollution: sector(s), rating, and 1-sentence justification in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will acknowledge the student’s strong draft, but I won’t rewrite it or provide the remaining rows. To follow the rules, I’ll use a focused Socratic prompt that helps them strengthen specificity and then produce the next row themselves. This keeps the interaction on the assignment, bite-sized, and moves directly toward completion without giving the answer.

exchanges.student: Air pollution — Health, environmental, and social — 8 — I gave it an 8 because exposure is widespread and ongoing in Jakarta, it creates a significant respiratory health burden, and lower-income communities may face higher exposure and fewer ways to reduce the risk.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. That’s defensible. Still, “may face higher exposure” is weaker than the rest—if you keep it, be ready to support it.

Finish the set: write your extreme heat row now with sector(s), rating, and 1-sentence justification.

After that, we can check whether your rankings are internally consistent.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student’s progress, point out one area needing sharper evidence-based specificity, and prompt them to complete the final stressor themselves. This follows the Socratic method, keeps the response brief, and avoids writing the assignment for them.

exchanges.student: Extreme heat — Health, social, and infrastructure — 7 — I gave it a 7 because Jakarta’s hot humid conditions and dense urban form can increase heat exposure, creating health risks for outdoor workers and low-income residents, while also increasing cooling demand.
exchanges.tutor:
This is good enough to submit as a basic set, but test it.

Ask yourself: why is air pollution 8 and extreme heat 7? What is the actual difference in scale, persistence, or citywide burden?

If you can defend that in 1–2 sentences, your table is coherent. Want to do that check now, or move to identifying priority stressors?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student’s progress, avoid giving a final polished solution, and use a brief Socratic check for internal consistency and assignment alignment. This keeps the student doing the reasoning and writing while nudging them toward a stronger final submission.
